title: PostgreSQL Intro
content:
PostgreSQL Introduction

What is PostgreSQL?

PostgreSQL (often called Postgres) is a powerful, open-source object-relational database system. It uses and extends the SQL language combined with many features that safely store and scale the most complicated data workloads.



  • ACID compliant
  • Advanced data types (JSON, Array)
  • Highly extensible
  • Thriving open-source community

title: PostgreSQL Setup
content:
PostgreSQL Installation

Getting Started

To use PostgreSQL on your local machine, you need to install the database server and a client tool like pgAdmin or psql (the command-line interface).

Connecting via psql
psql -U postgres
-- Prompts for password

-- Connect to a specific database:
\c mydatabase

title: Data Types
content:
PostgreSQL Data Types

Advanced Types

While Postgres supports standard SQL types (INTEGER, VARCHAR), it also shines with advanced, rich data types.

Table with Arrays & JSON
CREATE TABLE employees (
  id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
  name VARCHAR(100),
  tags TEXT[], -- Array of strings
  metadata JSONB -- Binary JSON format
);

title: Serial & Primary Keys
content:
SERIAL & Auto-Increment

Generating IDs

In MySQL you use AUTO_INCREMENT. In PostgreSQL, you use the SERIAL pseudo-type to automatically generate unique identifiers for your rows.

SERIAL Example
CREATE TABLE products (
  id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
  product_name VARCHAR(50)
);

-- No need to insert the ID!
INSERT INTO products (product_name) VALUES ('Keyboard');

title: RETURNING Clause
content:
The RETURNING Clause

Instant Feedback

PostgreSQL offers a fantastic feature not found in all SQL dialects: RETURNING. It allows you to output the data of the row you just inserted, updated, or deleted immediately.

RETURNING Example
INSERT INTO users (username, email)
VALUES ('johndoe', 'john@example.com')
RETURNING id, username;

title: UPSERT (ON CONFLICT)
content:
PostgreSQL UPSERT

Insert or Update

Sometimes you want to insert a row, but if it already exists (causes a unique constraint violation), you want to update it instead. This is called an UPSERT.

ON CONFLICT Example
INSERT INTO user_logins (user_id, login_count)
VALUES (1, 1)
ON CONFLICT (user_id) 
DO UPDATE SET login_count = user_logins.login_count + 1;

title: Array Data Types
content:
Querying Arrays

Arrays in SQL

Postgres lets you store arrays inside a column and query them directly using special operators like @> (contains) or && (overlaps).

Array Example
-- Find articles that have BOTH 'sql' and 'postgres' tags
SELECT title FROM articles
WHERE tags @> ARRAY['sql', 'postgres'];

title: JSONB Queries
content:
Querying JSONB

NoSQL Inside SQL

Postgres allows you to query deep inside JSON columns using special operators like -> and ->>.

JSON Query Example
-- Find all users where the JSON 'preferences' object has 'theme' set to 'dark'
SELECT name FROM users
WHERE preferences->>'theme' = 'dark';

title: Common Table Expressions
content:
PostgreSQL CTEs

Temporary Results with WITH

CTEs make complex queries infinitely more readable by creating temporary sub-query tables you can reference.

CTE Example
WITH active_users AS (
  SELECT id, name FROM users WHERE status = 'active'
)
SELECT * FROM active_users
JOIN orders ON orders.user_id = active_users.id;

title: Window Functions
content:
PostgreSQL Window Functions

Advanced Analytics

Window functions let you perform calculations across a set of table rows that are related to the current row, without collapsing the results like GROUP BY does.

OVER() Partitioning Example
-- Find the average salary per department alongside individual salaries
SELECT name, salary, department,
       AVG(salary) OVER (PARTITION BY department) AS dept_avg
FROM employees;

title: Full-Text Search
content:
Full-Text Search

Native Search Engine

PostgreSQL has powerful built-in text searching that supports stemming, ranking, and fuzzy matching via tsvector and tsquery.

FTS Example
-- Search for "jump" or "run" in a document
SELECT document_title
FROM documents
WHERE to_tsvector('english', document_body) @@ to_tsquery('jump | run');

title: Views & Materialized Views
content:
Materialized Views

Caching Query Results

Unlike standard views which run the underlying query every time, Materialized Views actually save the returned data to disk, serving as an intensive cache for complex metrics.

Materialized View Syntax
-- Create the cache table
CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW monthly_sales AS
SELECT EXTRACT(MONTH FROM sale_date) as mon, SUM(amount) 
FROM sales
GROUP BY 1;

-- Later, refresh the data!
REFRESH MATERIALIZED VIEW monthly_sales;

title: Advanced Indexes
content:
PostgreSQL Indexes

Beyond B-Trees

While Postgres defaults to B-Tree indexes, it offers specialized indexes for complex data types to dramatically speed up queries.

  • BRIN: Block Range Indexing, perfect for massive time-series data.
  • GiST: Generalized Search Tree, used for geometric data (PostGIS) and full-text search.
  • GIN: Generalized Inverted Index, essential for searching inside Arrays and JSONB documents.

title: JSONB Indexing
content:
Indexing JSONB

Lightning Fast NoSQL

Querying a JSON object column is slow on a million rows. You can create a GIN index on your JSONB column to make NoSQL-style document searches instantaneous.

GIN Index Syntax
-- Index the entirely of the JSON document for fast @> containment searches
CREATE INDEX idx_user_prefs_gin ON users USING GIN (preferences);

-- Or, index a specific nested JSON property as a standard B-Tree
CREATE INDEX idx_user_theme ON users ((preferences->>'theme'));

title: Roles & Permissions
content:
Roles & Grants

Database Security

Postgres uses "Roles" to manage both users and groups. You create a role, assign it a login password, and meticulously grant it exact access levels to specific tables.

GRANT Example
-- Create a restricted read-only user
CREATE ROLE reporting_user WITH LOGIN PASSWORD 'secure123';

-- Give them access to only SELECT data (no updates or deletes)
GRANT SELECT ON ALL TABLES IN SCHEMA public TO reporting_user;

title: Row-Level Security
content:
Row-Level Security (RLS)

Data Isolation

RLS allows you to tell the database: "Even if a user queries SELECT * FROM orders, only show them the orders that belong to their specific user ID." This enforces multi-tenant security directly at the database layer.

RLS Example
-- 1. Enable RLS on the table
ALTER TABLE orders ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY;

-- 2. Create the strict policy
CREATE POLICY user_orders_only ON orders 
FOR SELECT USING (user_id = current_user_id());

title: Advisory Locks
content:
Advisory Locks

Custom Concurrency

Standard SQL locks lock rows or tables. An Advisory Lock is an application-defined lock created purely by code. It has no meaning to the database, but your background workers (like Celery or Node.js jobs) can use it to prevent two workers from processing the exact same task simultaneously.

pg_advisory_lock
-- Acquire a lock using a custom ID number
SELECT pg_advisory_lock(12345);

-- ... your application does some long-running task ...

-- Release the lock for other workers
SELECT pg_advisory_unlock(12345);

title: Course Summary
content:
PostgreSQL Course Summary

PostgreSQL Excellence Achieved! 🎉

You have successfully completed the PostgreSQL course. You now understand how Postgres extends traditional SQL with powerful features.

  • Core Concepts: psql, SERIAL, UPSERT
  • Advanced Types: Arrays, JSONB operators
  • Analytics: CTEs, Window Functions, Materialized Views
  • Pro Features: RETURNING clause, Full-Text Search

You are now ready to build advanced backends with robust database architecture!
